message.content:
I need to build endpoint-to-scope mappings for Microsoft Graph API, specifically for Outlook (mail/calendar) and Microsoft Teams. These are used to map API requests to OAuth permission scopes for policy enforcement.

For each API, I need a JSON file with this structure:
```json
{
  "provider": "outlook",
  "apiHost": "graph.microsoft.com",
  "basePath": "/v1.0",
  "scopeMap": [
    {
      "method": "GET|POST|PUT|PATCH|DELETE",
      "pathPattern": "/v1.0/me/messages/*",
      "sufficientScopes": ["Mail.Read", "Mail.ReadWrite"],
      "description": "Get a message"
    }
  ],
  "allScopes": ["Mail.Read", "Mail.ReadWrite", ...]
}
```

## Outlook
Research the Microsoft Graph Mail and Calendar API endpoints. Key areas to cover:
- Messages (list, get, create, send, reply, forward, delete, move, copy)
- Mail folders (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Calendar events (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Calendars (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Calendar groups
- Contacts (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Contact folders
- Attachments
- Mail rules/message rules
- Focused inbox
- Search

Use Microsoft Graph permissions like: Mail.Read, Mail.ReadWrite, Mail.Send, Calendars.Read, Calendars.ReadWrite, Contacts.Read, Contacts.ReadWrite, MailboxSettings.Read, MailboxSettings.ReadWrite

Use `/v1.0/me/...` paths with `*` wildcards for IDs.

## Microsoft Teams
Research the Microsoft Graph Teams API endpoints:
- Teams (list, get, create, update, delete, archive)
- Channels (list, get, create, update, delete)
- Channel messages (list, get, send, reply)
- Chat (list, get, create, send message)
- Team members (list, get, add, remove)
- Apps/tabs in channels
- Meetings (if applicable via Teams API)

Use permissions like: Team.ReadBasic.All, TeamSettings.Read.All, TeamSettings.ReadWrite.All, Channel.ReadBasic.All, ChannelMessage.Read.All, ChannelMessage.Send, Chat.Read, Chat.ReadWrite, ChatMessage.Read, ChatMessage.Send, TeamsActivity.Read, TeamsActivity.Send, TeamMember.Read.All, TeamMember.ReadWrite.All

Search the web for "Microsoft Graph API mail permissions reference" and "Microsoft Graph Teams API permissions" to find the authoritative docs.

Path patterns should use `*` for IDs, e.g. `/v1.0/me/messages/*` or `/v1.0/teams/*/channels/*`.

Write:
- /workspace/scope-maps/outlook.json
- /workspace/scope-maps/microsoft_teams.json

Be thorough — cover all the major endpoints for each API.

Here's a summary of the **Microsoft Graph API Contacts Permissions Reference (v1.0)**:

## Contacts Permissions in Microsoft Graph v1.0

### Core Permissions

The Microsoft Graph API uses the following permissions for contacts operations:

**For Reading Contacts:**
- The `Contacts.Read` permission allows an application to access tenant data related to contacts.

**For Creating/Writing Contacts:**
- The required permissions for creating contacts are:
  - **Delegated (work or school account):** `Contacts.ReadWrite`
  - **Delegated (personal Microsoft account):** `Contacts.ReadWrite`
  - **Application:** `Contacts.ReadWrite`

**For Shared/Delegated Contacts:**
- The sharing permissions (`Contacts.Read.Shared` or `Contacts.ReadWrite.Shared`) allow you to read or write contacts in a shared or delegated folder.
- They do not support subscribing to change notifications on items in such folders.
- To set up change notification subscriptions on contacts in a shared, delegated, or any other user's contact folder in the tenant, use the application permission, `Contacts.Read`.

### API Endpoints (v1.0)

You can get contacts using the following endpoints:
- `GET /me/contacts` or `GET /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/contacts` — for the default Contacts folder
- `GET /me/contactfolders/{Id}/contacts` or `GET /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/contactfolders/{id}/contacts` — for contacts in a specific folder

### Best Practices

- Choose the permission or permissions marked as least privileged for the API. Use a higher privileged permission only if your app requires it.
- As a best practice, request the least privileged permissions that your app needs to access data and function correctly. Requesting permissions with more than the necessary privileges is poor security practice, which may cause users to refrain from consenting.

### Permission Types

- Microsoft Graph supports two access scenarios: **delegated access** and **app-only access**. In delegated access, the app calls Microsoft Graph on behalf of a signed-in user. In app-only access, the app calls Microsoft Graph with its own identity, without a signed-in user.

### Setting Up Application Access

- To access user contacts, you need to register the application in Microsoft Entra ID and grant the `Contacts.Read` permission of the appropriate type (e.g., Application type for app-only access).

### Key Resources
- **Full Permissions Reference:** [Microsoft Graph permissions reference](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/permissions-reference)
- **List Contacts API (v1.0):** [List contacts - Microsoft Graph v1.0](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/api/user-list-contacts?view=graph-rest-1.0)
- **Get Contact API (v1.0):** [Get contact - Microsoft Graph v1.0](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/api/contact-get?view=graph-rest-1.0)
- **Shared Contacts Guide:** [Get Outlook contacts in a shared folder](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/outlook-get-shared-contacts-folders)

Here's a summary of what I found about Microsoft Graph API calendar permissions in v1.0:

## Microsoft Graph API — Calendar Permissions Reference (v1.0)

### calendarPermission Resource Type

The `calendarPermission` resource type represents the permissions of a user with whom the calendar has been shared or delegated in an Outlook client. List, create, get, update, and delete of calendar permissions is supported on behalf of only the calendar owner.

### Calendar Sharing Overview

When users share calendars with other users from within Outlook clients, they can control the calendar items that the recipients can view or edit. The `calendarPermissions` relationship contains permissions for every user with whom a user shared their calendar. This relationship allows you to, for example, see which users can view free/busy information for the owner, view all calendar information, or edit events on the calendar.

### API Operations & Required Permissions

For all calendar permission operations, depending on the type of calendar that the event is in and the permission type (delegated or application) requested, one of the following permissions is required to call the API.

Key operations include:

1. **Get calendarPermission** — Gets the specified permissions object of a user or group calendar that has been shared. If successful, this method returns a `200 OK` response code and the requested `calendarPermission` object in the response body.

2. **Create calendarPermission** — In the request body, supply a JSON representation of a `calendarPermission` object. If successful, this method returns a `200 OK` response code and a collection of `calendarPermission` objects in the response body.

3. **Update calendarPermission** — Updates the permissions assigned to an existing share recipient or delegate, through the corresponding `calendarPermission` object for a calendar.

### HTTP Endpoints

For **getting/updating** calendar permissions:
- User's calendar: `GET/PATCH /users/{id}/calendar/calendarPermissions/{id}`
- Group calendar: `PATCH /groups/{id}/calendar/calendarPermissions/{id}`

For **creating** calendar permissions:
- Example: `POST https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me/calendar/calendarPermissions` with a body including `emailAddress`, `isInsideOrganization`, `isRemovable`, and `role`.

### Calendar Permission Roles

The `role` property uses the `calendarRoleType` enum. For example, you can change the permission level of a share recipient to "write" via a PATCH request.

### Key Permissions (Scopes) for Calendar APIs

Getting the user's calendar data requires `Calendars.Read`, while updating the calendar with scheduled jobs requires a higher privileged permission, `Calendars.ReadWrite`. In this case, the app should request `Calendars.ReadWrite`.

For application (app-only) permissions:
- Visibility of event subject and location depends on whether you use permissions such as `Calendars.Read.All` or `Calendars.ReadBasic.All`.
- The `Calendars.Read.All` permission allows access to appointments even if they are marked as private.

### Best Practices

As a best practice, request the least privileged permissions that your app needs in order to access data and function correctly. Requesting permissions with more than the necessary privileges is poor security practice, which may cause users to refrain from consenting and affect your app's usage.

### Official Documentation Links

- **calendarPermission resource type**: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/api/resources/calendarpermission?view=graph-rest-1.0
- **Get calendarPermission**: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/api/calendarpermission-get?view=graph-rest-1.0
- **Create calendarPermission**: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/api/calendar-post-calendarpermissions?view=graph-rest-1.0
- **Update calendarPermission**: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/api/calendarpermission-update?view=graph-rest-1.0
- **Full permissions reference**: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/permissions-reference

## Key Resources Found

### 1. Microsoft Graph Permissions Reference (Official Documentation)
**Source:** [Microsoft Graph permissions reference – Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/permissions-reference)

Group.* permissions are used to control access to Microsoft Teams resources and APIs. Key points include:

- For Microsoft 365 groups, Group.* permissions grant the app access to the contents of the group; for example, conversations, files, notes, and so on.
- In some cases, an app might need extra permissions to read some group properties like member and memberOf. For example, if a group has one or more service principals as members, the app also needs permissions to read service principals, otherwise Microsoft Graph returns an error or limited information.
- To read information about all Microsoft Graph permissions programmatically, sign in to an API client such as Graph Explorer using an account that has at least the Application.Read.All permission and run `GET https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals(appId='00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000')?$select=id,appId,displayName,appRoles,oauth2PermissionScopes,resourceSpecificApplicationPermissions`.

### 2. Overview of Microsoft Graph Permissions
**Source:** [Overview of Microsoft Graph permissions – Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/permissions-overview)

- Microsoft Graph exposes granular permissions that allow an app to request only the permissions it needs to function. Granular permissions let you apply the principle of least privilege when assigning and granting permissions to an app.
- RSC permissions are also available for consent and are supported by only a subset of features available through Microsoft Graph such as Teams, chats, and messages.

### 3. Teams API Overview (v1.0)
**Source:** [Use the Microsoft Graph API to work with Microsoft Teams – Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/api/resources/teams-api-overview?view=graph-rest-1.0)

Microsoft Teams is a chat-based workspace in Microsoft 365 that provides built-in access to team-specific calendars, files, OneNote notes, Planner plans, and more.

### 4. Specific API Permission Examples

**Create Team (POST /teams):**
- The Teamwork.Migrate.All permission is only supported for migration.
- The Group.ReadWrite.All and Directory.ReadWrite.All permissions are supported only for backward compatibility. We recommend that you update your solutions to use an alternative permission.

**Get Team (GET /teams/{id}):**
- This API supports admin permissions. Microsoft Teams service admins can access teams that they aren't a member of.
- The TeamSettings.Read.Group and TeamSettings.ReadWrite.Group permission use resource-specific consent.
- The Group.Read.All, Group.ReadWrite.All, Directory.Read.All, and Directory.ReadWrite.All permissions are supported only for backward compatibility. We recommend that you update your solutions to use an alternative permission.

### 5. Best Practices for Permissions
**Source:** [Best practices for using Microsoft Graph permissions – Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/best-practices-graph-permission)

- Apps published on the Teams store can use the app persistent ID instead of using AppCatalog.Read.All to get the app ID.
- Apps that send messages to a chat can use ChatMessage.Send.Chat RSC permission instead of the Chat.ReadWrite delegated Microsoft Graph permission.
- By requesting the fewest, least-privileged permissions required for your app, you apply the principle of least privilege, which builds trust with customer tenant admins.

### 6. Chat Messages Permissions
For reading Teams chat messages:
- You need Chat.Read or Chat.ReadWrite permissions.
- Use ClientSecretCredential with Chat.Read.All and admin consent for background apps.

### 7. What's New (2025 Updates)
**Source:** [What's new in Microsoft Graph – Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/whats-new-overview)

Recent Teams-related updates include:
- Get the policy ID for a given policy name and policy type within Teams administration. Assign a Teams policy to a user using the user ID, policy type, and policy ID. Unassign a Teams policy from a user using the user ID and policy type.
- Assign a Teams telephone number to a user account. Unassign a Teams telephone number from a user account.

For the full and most up-to-date permissions reference, visit the official documentation at **https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/permissions-reference**.

## Microsoft Graph API — Mail Permissions Reference (v1.0)

### Overview

Microsoft Graph lets your app get authorized access to a user's Outlook mail data in a personal or organization account. With the appropriate delegated or application mail permissions, your app can access the mail data of the signed-in user.

With the appropriate delegated or application mail permissions, your app can access the mail data of the signed-in user or any user in a tenant.

### Mailbox Access

The Microsoft Graph API supports accessing data in users' primary mailboxes and in shared mailboxes. The data can be calendar, mail, or personal contacts stored in a mailbox in the cloud on Exchange Online as part of Microsoft 365, or on Exchange on-premises in a hybrid deployment. The API does not support accessing in-place archive mailboxes, not on Exchange Online nor on Exchange Server.

Mail API requests are performed on behalf of a user which can be identified by the user's id property (a unique GUID), email address, or the `me` shortcut alias for the signed-in user.

### Key Mail Permissions

The main mail-related permissions in Microsoft Graph include:

- **Mail.Read** — Read user mail (delegated & application)
- **Mail.ReadWrite** — Read and write access to user mail
- **Mail.Send** — Send mail as a user
- **Mail.ReadBasic** — Read basic mail properties
- **MailboxSettings.Read** — Read user mailbox settings
- **MailboxSettings.ReadWrite** — Read and write user mailbox settings

### Delegated vs. Application Permissions

When you go through the process of creating an app registration with the necessary Graph API permissions, you encounter two types of application permissions: delegated permissions are used by apps that have a signed-in user present. They allow the app to act on behalf of the user and access resources based on the user's permissions. Examples include reading the signed-in user's profile or accessing their mailbox.

Application permissions are used by apps that run without a signed-in user. They allow the app to access resources directly and are suitable for background services, websites, scripts, automation, etc. Examples of potential actions include accessing all users' profiles or emails within an organization or sending emails on behalf of any user.

### Application Permission Scope Concerns

MailboxSettings.ReadWrite is an application permission, which by default allows access to all mailboxes in the tenant.

If we examine the permission set of the Microsoft Graph API and look at the application permission Mail.Send, we can see that the description of this permission states, "Send mail as any user."

### Restricting Application Permissions to Specific Mailboxes

Graph API can work either on delegated permission or application-level permissions. However, you can limit application permissions to a specific group/users by creating a `New-ApplicationAccessPolicy`.

The only supported way to constrain Graph mailbox access is still Application Access Policies (AAP), even though Microsoft labels it as "legacy."

### Sending Mail via Graph API (Application Permissions)

You assign Mail.Send (Application) permission and grant admin consent. Once this is done, the app can send mail from the shared mailbox using Graph: `POST https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{sharedMailbox}/sendMail`. No "Send As" configuration is needed in Exchange, because the app uses Graph permissions, not mailbox delegation.

You may get a 401 error because the user you are sending from does not have an Exchange Online mailbox. Even with Mail.Send and admin consent, Graph cannot send email from an address that isn't backed by a mailbox. An Azure subscription does not include email.

### New Granular Permissions (2025)

In January 2025, Microsoft released a set of new, more granular Graph API permissions. The new scope **User-Mail.ReadWrite.All** allows you to manage the `otherMails` attribute for the user. This property allows you to set the alternate email address that's mandatory for admin role assignments, and is also used as part of SSPR.

### Best Practices

As a best practice, request the least privileged permissions that your app needs in order to access data and function correctly.

Requesting permissions with more than the necessary privileges is poor security practice, which may cause users to refrain from consenting and affect your app's usage.

### Key Resources

- **Permissions Reference**: [Microsoft Graph permissions reference](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/permissions-reference)
- **Mail API Overview (v1.0)**: [Use the Outlook mail REST API](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/api/resources/mail-api-overview?view=graph-rest-1.0)
- **Permissions Overview**: [Overview of Microsoft Graph permissions](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/permissions-overview)

# Use the Outlook mail REST API - Microsoft Graph v1.0 | Microsoft Learn

Microsoft Graph lets your app get authorized access to a user's Outlook mail data in a personal or organization account. With the appropriate delegated or application [mail permissions](/en-us/graph/permissions-reference#mail-permissions), your app can access the mail data of the signed-in user or any user in a tenant. For more information on access tokens, app registration, and delegated and application permissions, see [Authentication and authorization basics](/en-us/graph/auth/auth-concepts).

The Microsoft Graph API supports accessing data in users' *primary* mailboxes and in [shared mailboxes](https://support.office.com/article/open-and-use-a-shared-mailbox-in-outlook-d94a8e9e-21f1-4240-808b-de9c9c088afd). The data can be calendar, mail, or personal contacts stored in a mailbox in the cloud on Exchange Online as part of Microsoft 365, or on Exchange on-premises in a [hybrid deployment](/en-us/graph/hybrid-rest-support).

The API does *not* support accessing in-place archive mailboxes, not [on Exchange Online](/en-us/microsoft-365/compliance/enable-archive-mailboxes) nor [on Exchange Server](/en-us/Exchange/policy-and-compliance/in-place-archiving/in-place-archiving?view=exchserver-2019&preserve-view=true).

## Using the mail REST API

Mail API requests are performed on behalf of a [user](user) which can be identified by the user's **id** property (a unique GUID), email address, or the `me` shortcut alias for the signed-in user.

Email messages are represented by the [message](message) resource and organized in a [mailFolder](mailfolder). Messages and mail folders are identified by their **id** property, obtainable from `GET` operations.

Important

In general, do not assume that **message** and **mailfolder** IDs are unique and always remain the same within a mailbox. They might change after certain actions such as copy or move. You can choose to use [immutable IDs](/en-us/graph/outlook-immutable-id) to retain the same ID as long as the message remains in the same mailbox, *with the exception of sending a draft message, and a few other scenarios*. See [lifetime of immutable IDs](/en-us/graph/outlook-immutable-id#lifetime-of-immutable-ids) for details.

Message bodies can be in HTML or text format.

You can use well-known folder names such as `Inbox`, `Drafts`, `SentItems`, or `DeletedItems` to identify certain mail folders that exist by default for all users. For a list of supported well-known folder names, see [mailFolder resource type](mailfolder).

For example, you can get messages in the Outlook **Sent Items** folder of the signed-in user, without first getting the folder ID:

```http
GET /me/mailFolders('SentItems')/messages?$select=sender,subject
```

## Common use cases

The **message** resource exposes properties such as **categories**, **conversationId**, **flag**, and **importance** that correspond to features available in the UI, allowing apps to automate or integrate with the built-in Outlook user experience.

The Microsoft Graph API also provides methods and actions that support common use cases of messages.

| Use cases | REST resources | See also |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **User-centric actions** |  |  |
| Draft, read, reply, forward, send, update, or delete messages | [message](message) | [Methods of message](message#methods) |
| Delegate another user to send messages on behalf of the mailbox owner | [message](message) | Setting the **from** and **sender** properties in a [message](message) |
| Let user view more important messages first | [inferenceClassificationOverride](inferenceclassificationoverride) | [Focused Inbox](manage-focused-inbox) |
| Query for messages and get them in a search folder | [mailSearchFolder](mailsearchfolder) | [Methods of mailSearchFolder](mailsearchfolder#methods) |
| Get the MIME content of a message or message attachment | [message](message) | [Get MIME content](/en-us/graph/outlook-get-mime-message) |

| Add, get, or delete attachments of a message | [attachment](attachment), [fileAttachment](fileattachment), [itemAttachment](itemattachment), [referenceAttachment](referenceattachment), [message](message) | [Methods of attachment](attachment#methods) |

# Working with calendars and events using the Microsoft Graph API - Microsoft Graph v1.0 | Microsoft Learn

The Microsoft Graph Calendar API provides [calendar](calendar), [calendarGroup](calendargroup), [event](event), and other resources that enable you to create events and meetings, find workable meeting times, manage attendees, and more. With the Calendar API, you can build a variety of experiences with calendar data.

## Manage events and meetings

The [event](event) type represents a scheduled occurrence on a calendar, such as a meeting, holiday, or time block. Meetings, such as team meetings or one-on-ones, are all represented by **event** resources. You can directly manage the event lifecycle by creating, canceling, and deleting events directly, among other actions. Also, you can create draft event messages, send them, forward them, and create draft replies, and more. By working with event messages, you enable the user to take an active role in creating events and meetings. You also enable them to communicate to meeting originators, other recipients, and attendees.

### Working directly with events

The Microsoft Graph API provides methods for operations such as creating, updating, deleting, and canceling events. The following table lists some common lifecycle event use cases and the APIs that Microsoft Graph provides for working with them.

| Use case | Verb | Example URL |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Create an event.](../user-post-events) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/events |
| [Delete an event from a calendar.](../event-delete) | DELETE | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/events/{id} |
| [Cancel an event and send a cancellation message.](../event-cancel)**Note**: Specify the optional cancellation message in the request body. | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/events/{id}/cancel |
| [Update an event.](../event-update)**Note**: Specify the event details to update in the [request body](../event-update#request-body). | PATCH | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/events/{id} |
| [Accept an event.](../event-accept) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/events/{id}/accept |
| [Tentatively accept an event.](../event-tentativelyaccept) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/events/{id}/tentativelyAccept |
| [Decline an event.](../event-decline) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/events/{id}/decline |
| [Dismiss an event reminder.](../event-dismissreminder) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/events/{id}/dismissReminder |
| [Snooze an event reminder.](../event-snoozereminder) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/events/{id}/snoozeReminder |

### Working with event messages

The [eventMessage](eventmessage) resource is an abstract type that represents meeting requests, cancellations, and responses. Responses are generated when the message recipient accepts, tentatively accepts, or declines the request. Handling [eventMessageRequest](eventmessagerequest) and [eventMessageResponse](eventmessageresponse) moves the event through its lifecycle. The messaging APIs in the Calendar API support both MIME and JSON content.

The following table lists some common event message use cases and the APIs for working with them.

| Use case | Verb | Example URL |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Send an existing draft message.](../message-send) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages/{id}/send |
| [Create a draft reply.](../message-createreply) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages/{id}/createReply |
| [Reply to an event message.](../message-reply) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages/{id}/reply |
| [Create a draft reply-all message.](../message-createreplyall) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages/{id}/createReplyAll |
| [Reply to all in an event message.](../message-replyall) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages/{id}/replyAll |
| [Create a draft forward.](../message-createforward) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages/{id}/createForward |
| [Forward an event message.](../message-forward) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages/{id}/forward |

## Adding and removing attachments

The abstract [attachment](attachment) type serves as a base for files, items, and references that are attached to events, messages, and posts. You can view the attachments for an event, for example, with the [List attachments](../event-list-attachments) method. You can delete an attachment with the [Delete attachment](../attachment-delete) method. Events in group calendars don't support attachments.

### Attachment types

The [fileAttachment](fileattachment), [itemAttachment](itemattachment), and [referenceAttatchment](referenceattachment) types represent the three kinds of items that can be attached to calendar items. An **itemAttachment** object represents a contact, event, or message that is directly attached to a user event, message, or post. A **fileAttachment** represents a file that is directly attached. A **referenceAttachment** represents an item, such as a Word document or text file, that is located on a OneDrive for work or school cloud drive or other supported storage location. To see all of the attachments for an [event](event), for example, you can use the [GET /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/events/{id}/attachments](../event-list-attachments) endpoint.

### Uploading attachments

You can directly upload attachments less than 3 MB in size to an event for a user with the [Add attachment](../event-post-attachments) method. For an attachment that is larger than 3 MB, however, you must use the [attachment: createUploadSession](../attachment-createuploadsession) method to get an upload URL that you use to iteratively upload the attachment.

## Work with calendars, calendar groups, and Outlook categories

With the Calendar API, you can create, read, update, and delete calendars, create and view calendar events, get free/busy information for users, and find suggested meeting times.

The Calendar API provides methods to operate on calendars and calendar groups. The following table shows some use cases with selected URLs.

> 
> **Note**: Many of the methods shown in the following table have other URLs for related use cases. For example, to update a user's calendar in a specific calendar group, send a PATCH operation with the URL `/users/{id | userPrincipalName}/calendarGroups/{id}/calendars/{id}`.

| Use case | Verb | Example URL |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [List calendars for a user.](../user-list-calendars) | GET | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/calendars |
| [List a user's calendars in a group.](../user-list-calendars) | GET | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/calendarGroups/{calendarGroupId}/calendars |
| [Create a calendar.](../user-post-calendars) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/calendars |
| [Get a calendar.](../calendar-get) | GET | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/calendars/{id} |
| [Update a calendar.](../calendar-update) | PATCH | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/calendars/{id} |
| [Delete a calendar.](../calendar-delete) | DELETE | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/calendars/{id} |
| [Create a calendar group.](../user-post-calendargroups) | POST | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/calendarGroups |
| [Get a calendar group.](../calendargroup-get) | GET | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/calendarGroups/{id} |
| [Update a calendar group.](../calendargroup-update) | PATCH | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/calendarGroups/{id} |
| [Delete a calendar group.](../calendargroup-delete) | DELETE | /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/calendarGroups/{id} |

### Free/busy data and meeting times

Two of the core functions of calendaring are to find free/busy information and find meeting times in order to schedule meetings. The Calendar API provides the [Get free/busy schedule](../calendar-getschedule) method that returns a collection of [scheduleInformation](scheduleinformation) objects for a time period and a collection of users, resources, or distribution lists. You can present this information to the user so that they can manually pick an appropriate time at which to schedule a meeting. Use the [user: findMeetingTimes](../user-findmeetingtimes) method to get a [meetingTimeSuggestionResult](meetingtimesuggestionsresult) that contains a collection of [meetingTimeSuggestion](meetingtimesuggestion) objects that represent detailed information about proposed meeting times for the participants and constraints that you sent.

### Outlook categories

A calendar category is a combination of a description and a **categoryColor** that together define a category for an Outlook item and control how Outlook displays the item. Outlook users can group messages and events, for example, by category. For more information, see [outlookCategory](outlookcategory).

### Calendar permissions

When users share calendars with other users from within Outlook clients, they can control the calendar items that the recipients can view or edit. The [calendarPermissions](calendar#relationships) relationship contains permissions for every user with whom a user shared their calendar. This relationship allows you to, for example, see which users can view free/busy information for the owner, view all calendar information, or edit events on the calendar.

## Work with open extensions and extended properties

[Open extensions](opentypeextension), formerly known as Office 365 data extensions, represent the preferred way to store and access custom data for resources in a user's mailbox. If an Outlook MAPI property isn't available in the Microsoft Graph API metadata, then you can fall back to Outlook extended properties. For more information, see [Outlook extended properties overview](extended-properties-overview).

# Use the Microsoft Graph API to work with Microsoft Teams - Microsoft Graph v1.0 | Microsoft Learn

Microsoft Teams is a chat-based workspace in Microsoft 365 that provides built-in access to team-specific calendars, files, OneNote notes, Planner plans, Shifts schedules, and more. You can use the Microsoft Graph API to integrate with Microsoft Teams features.

## Common use cases

The following table lists common use cases for Microsoft Teams APIs in Microsoft Graph.

| Use cases | REST resources | See also |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Create and manage teams, groups, and channels | [team](team), [channel](channel), [group](group) | [create team](../team-put-teams), [list teams](../user-list-joinedteams), [create channel](../channel-post) |
| Add tabs, manage, or install apps in the Microsoft Teams app catalog | [teamsTab](teamstab), [teamsAppInstallation](teamsappinstallation) | [create teamsTab](../channel-post-tabs), [list teamsTab](../channel-list-tabs), [list apps](../appcatalogs-list-teamsapps) |
| Create channels and chats to send and receive chat messages | [channel](channel), [chat](chat), [chatMessage](chatmessage) | [create channel](../channel-post), [list channel](../channel-list), [send chatMessage](../chatmessage-post) |
| Use tags to classify users or groups based on common attributes within a team | [teamworkTag](teamworktag), [teamworkTagMember](teamworktagmember) | [list teamworkTag](../teamworktag-list), [create teamworkTag](../teamworktag-post) |
| Create and receive calls, call records or retrieve meeting coordinates | [call](call), [callRecords](callrecords-api-overview) | [answer](../call-answer), [invite participants](../participant-invite) |
| Connect bots to calls and implement interactive voice response (IVR) | [call](call) | IVR scenarios |
| Create and retrieve online meetings or check users presence and activity | [onlineMeetings](onlinemeeting), [presence](presence) | [create onlineMeetings](../application-post-onlinemeetings), [meetingAttendanceReport](meetingattendancereport) |
| Create and manage workforce integration with shifts, schedules, time cards, or time off in your organization | [workforceIntegration](workforceintegration), [schedule](schedule), [shift](shift), [timeOff](timeoff), [timeOffReason](timeoffreason) | [create workforceIntegration](../workforceintegration-post), [create schedule](../schedule-post-schedulinggroups), [create shift](../schedule-post-shifts), [create timeOff](../schedule-post-timesoff) |
| Use the employee learning API to integrate with Viva Learning | [employee learning](viva-learning-api-overview), [learningProvider](learningprovider), [learningContent](learningcontent) | [list learningProviders](../employeeexperience-list-learningproviders), [list learningContents](../learningprovider-list-learningcontents) |

### IVR scenarios

The following are the Interactive Voice Response (IVR) scenarios that the calling APIs in Microsoft Graph support:

- [Play an audio prompt](/en-us/graph/api/call-playprompt) - for example, when a call is placed in a customer service agent's queue.
- [Record a response](/en-us/graph/api/call-record) - for example, to record the caller's audio, usually after they heard a prompt with options.
- [Subscribe to tones](/en-us/graph/api/call-subscribetotone) - for example, when you want to know what DTMF tones the caller selected, usually after hearing the audio prompt.
- [Cancel media processing](/en-us/graph/api/call-cancelmediaprocessing) - for example, when you want to cancel any **playPrompt** or **recordResponse** operations that might be in process.

## Microsoft Teams limits

The tested performance and capacity limits of Microsoft Teams are documented in [Limits and specifications for Microsoft Teams](/en-us/microsoftteams/limits-specifications-teams). These limits apply whether using Microsoft Teams directly or using Microsoft Graph APIs. Because every team has a corresponding group, and every group is a directory object, limits on the [number of groups](/en-us/microsoft-365/admin/create-groups/office-365-groups#group-limits) and the [number of directory objects ("resources")](/en-us/azure/active-directory/users-groups-roles/directory-service-limits-restrictions) can also come into play.

Files inside channels are stored in SharePoint; [SharePoint online limits](/en-us/office365/servicedescriptions/sharepoint-online-service-description/sharepoint-online-limits) apply.

See also [throttling limits for Microsoft Teams services](/en-us/graph/throttling#microsoft-teams-service-limits).

## Teams and groups

In Microsoft Graph, Microsoft Teams is represented by a [group](group) resource. Both Microsoft Teams and Microsoft 365 groups address the various needs of group collaboration. Almost all the group-based features apply to Microsoft Teams and Microsoft 365 groups, such as group calendar, files, notes, photo, plans, and so on. The main difference between a [team](team) and a Microsoft 365 group is the mode of communication between members. Team members communicate by persistent chat in the context of a specific team. Microsoft 365 group members communicate by group conversations, which are email conversations that occur in the context of a group in Outlook.

Any group that has a team has a **resourceProvisioningOptions** property that contains "Team".

> 
> **Note:** The **Group.resourceProvisioningOptions** property can be changed. Do not add or remove "Team" from that collection; otherwise, you'll get incorrect results when you list all teams.

The following are the differences at the API level between teams and groups:

- Persistent chat is available only to Microsoft Teams. This feature is hierarchically represented by the [channel](channel) and [chatMessage](chatmessage) resources.
- Group conversations are available only to Microsoft 365 groups. This feature is hierarchically represented by the [conversation](conversation), [conversationThread](conversationthread), and [post](post) resources.
- The [List joined teams](../user-list-joinedteams) method applies only to Microsoft Teams.
- [Calling](call) and [online meeting](onlinemeeting) APIs apply only to Microsoft Teams.
- See also the [known issues](https://developer.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/known-issues) for these APIs.

## Membership changes in Microsoft Teams

| Use case | Verb | URL |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Add member](../team-post-members) | POST | /teams/{team-id}/members |
| [Remove member](../team-delete-members) | DELETE | /teams/{team-id}/members/{membership-id} |
| [Update member's role](../team-update-members) | PATCH | /teams/{team-id}/members/{membership-id} |
| [Update team](../team-update) | PATCH | /teams/{team-id} |

## Polling requirements

If your app polls to see whether a resource has changed, you can only do that once per day. ([teamsAsyncOperation](teamsasyncoperation) is an exception in that it's intended to be polled frequently.) If you need to hear about changes more frequently than that, you should [create a subscription](../subscription-post-subscriptions) to that resource and receive change notifications (webhooks). If you don't find support for the type of subscription you need, we encourage you to provide feedback via the [Microsoft 365 Developer Platform ideas forum](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/microsoft-365-developer-platform/idb-p/Microsoft365DeveloperPlatform/label-name/Microsoft%20Graph).

When polling for new messages, you must specify a date range where supported. For more information, see [get delta chat messages for a user](../chatmessage-delta).

Polling is doing a GET operation on a resource over and over again to see if that resource has changed. You're allowed to GET the same resource multiple times a day, as long as it's not polling. For example, it's okay to GET /me/joinedTeams every time the user visits/refreshes your web page, but it isn't okay to GET /me/joinedTeams in a loop every 30 seconds to refresh that web page.

Apps that don't follow these polling requirements will be considered in violation of the [Microsoft APIs Terms of Use](/en-us/legal/microsoft-apis/terms-of-use). This may result in additional [throttling](/en-us/graph/throttling) or the suspension or termination of your use of the Microsoft APIs.

# List messages - Microsoft Graph v1.0 | Microsoft Learn

Namespace: microsoft.graph

Get the messages in the signed-in user's mailbox (including the Deleted Items and Clutter folders).

Depending on the page size and mailbox data, getting messages from a mailbox can incur multiple requests. The default page size is 10 messages. Use `$top` to customize the page size, within the range of 1 and 1000.

To improve the operation response time, use `$select` to specify the exact properties you need; see example 1 below. Fine-tune the values for `$select` and `$top`, especially when you must use a larger page size, as returning a page with hundreds of messages each with a full response payload may trigger the [gateway timeout](/en-us/graph/errors#http-status-codes) (HTTP 504).

To get the next page of messages, simply apply the entire URL returned in `@odata.nextLink` to the next get-messages request. This URL includes any query parameters you may have specified in the initial request.

Do not try to extract the `$skip` value from the `@odata.nextLink` URL to manipulate responses. This API uses the `$skip` value to keep count of all the items it has gone through in the user's mailbox to return a page of message-type items. It's therefore possible that even in the initial response, the `$skip` value is larger than the page size. For more information, see [Paging Microsoft Graph data in your app](/en-us/graph/paging).

Currently, this operation returns message bodies in only HTML format.

There are two scenarios where an app can get messages in another user's mail folder:

- If the app has application permissions, or,
- If the app has the appropriate delegated permissions from one user, and another user has shared a mail folder with that user, or, has given delegated access to that user. See [details and an example](/en-us/graph/outlook-share-messages-folders).

This API is available in the following [national cloud deployments](/en-us/graph/deployments).

| Global service | US Government L4 | US Government L5 (DOD) | China operated by 21Vianet |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

## Permissions

Choose the permission or permissions marked as least privileged for this API. Use a higher privileged permission or permissions [only if your app requires it](/en-us/graph/permissions-overview#best-practices-for-using-microsoft-graph-permissions). For details about delegated and application permissions, see [Permission types](/en-us/graph/permissions-overview#permission-types). To learn more about these permissions, see the [permissions reference](/en-us/graph/permissions-reference).

| Permission type | Least privileged permissions | Higher privileged permissions |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Delegated (work or school account) | Mail.ReadBasic | Mail.ReadWrite, Mail.Read |
| Delegated (personal Microsoft account) | Mail.ReadBasic | Mail.ReadWrite, Mail.Read |
| Application | Mail.ReadBasic.All | Mail.ReadWrite, Mail.Read |

## HTTP request

To get all the messages in a user's mailbox:

```http
GET /me/messages
GET /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages
```

To get messages in a specific folder in the user's mailbox:

```http
GET /me/mailFolders/{id}/messages
GET /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/mailFolders/{id}/messages
```

## Optional query parameters

This method supports the [OData Query Parameters](/en-us/graph/query-parameters) to help customize the response.

### Using filter and orderby in the same query

When using `$filter` and `$orderby` in the same query to get messages, make sure to specify properties in the following ways:

1. Properties that appear in `$orderby` must also appear in `$filter`.
2. Properties that appear in `$orderby` are in the same order as in `$filter`.
3. Properties that are present in `$orderby` appear in `$filter` before any properties that aren't.

Failing to do this results in the following error:

- Error code: `InefficientFilter`
- Error message: `The restriction or sort order is too complex for this operation.`

## Request headers

| Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Authorization | string | Bearer {token}. Required. Learn more about [authentication and authorization](/en-us/graph/auth/auth-concepts). |
| Prefer: outlook.body-content-type | string | The format of the **body** and **uniqueBody** properties to be returned in. Values can be "text" or "html". If the header is not specified, the **body** and **uniqueBody** properties are returned in HTML format. Optional. |

## Request body

Don't supply a request body for this method.

## Response

If successful, this method returns a `200 OK` response code and collection of [Message](resources/message) objects in the response body.

## Examples

### Example 1: List all messages

#### Request

The following shows an example that gets the default, top 10 messages in the signed-in user's mailbox. It uses `$select` to return a subset of the properties of each message in the response.

**HTTP**

```msgraph
GET https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me/messages?$select=sender,subject
```

**C#**

```csharp

// Code snippets are only available for the latest version. Current version is 5.x

// To initialize your graphClient, see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/sdks/create-client?from=snippets&tabs=csharp
var result = await graphClient.Me.Messages.GetAsync((requestConfiguration) =>
{requestConfiguration.QueryParameters.Select = new string []{ "sender","subject" };
});

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

**Go**

```go

// Code snippets are only available for the latest major version. Current major version is $v1.*

// Dependencies
import (  "context"  msgraphsdk "github.com/microsoftgraph/msgraph-sdk-go"  graphusers "github.com/microsoftgraph/msgraph-sdk-go/users"  //other-imports
)

requestParameters := &graphusers.ItemMessagesRequestBuilderGetQueryParameters{Select: [] string {"sender","subject"},
}
configuration := &graphusers.ItemMessagesRequestBuilderGetRequestConfiguration{QueryParameters: requestParameters,
}

// To initialize your graphClient, see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/sdks/create-client?from=snippets&tabs=go
messages, err := graphClient.Me().Messages().Get(context.Background(), configuration)

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

**Java**

```java

// Code snippets are only available for the latest version. Current version is 6.x

GraphServiceClient graphClient = new GraphServiceClient(requestAdapter);

MessageCollectionResponse result = graphClient.me().messages().get(requestConfiguration -> {requestConfiguration.queryParameters.select = new String []{"sender", "subject"};
});

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

**JavaScript**

```javascript

const options = {authProvider,
};

const client = Client.init(options);

let messages = await client.api('/me/messages').select('sender,subject').get();

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

**PHP**

```php

<?php
use Microsoft\Graph\GraphServiceClient;
use Microsoft\Graph\Generated\Users\Item\Messages\MessagesRequestBuilderGetRequestConfiguration;

$graphServiceClient = new GraphServiceClient($tokenRequestContext, $scopes);

$requestConfiguration = new MessagesRequestBuilderGetRequestConfiguration();
$queryParameters = MessagesRequestBuilderGetRequestConfiguration::createQueryParameters();
$queryParameters->select = ["sender","subject"];
$requestConfiguration->queryParameters = $queryParameters;

$result = $graphServiceClient->me()->messages()->get($requestConfiguration)->wait();

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

**PowerShell**

```powershell

Import-Module Microsoft.Graph.Mail

# A UPN can also be used as -UserId.
Get-MgUserMessage -UserId $userId -Property "sender,subject" 

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

**Python**

```python

# Code snippets are only available for the latest version. Current version is 1.x
from msgraph import GraphServiceClient
from msgraph.generated.users.item.messages.messages_request_builder import MessagesRequestBuilder
from kiota_abstractions.base_request_configuration import RequestConfiguration
# To initialize your graph_client, see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/sdks/create-client?from=snippets&tabs=python
query_params = MessagesRequestBuilder.MessagesRequestBuilderGetQueryParameters(	select = ["sender","subject"],
)

request_configuration = RequestConfiguration(
query_parameters = query_params,
)

result = await graph_client.me.messages.get(request_configuration = request_configuration)

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

#### Response

The following example shows the response. To get the next page of messages, apply the URL returned in `@odata.nextLink` to a subsequent GET request.

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-type: application/json

{
    "@odata.context": "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/$metadata#users('bb8775a4-4d8c-42cf-a1d4-4d58c2bb668f')/messages(sender,subject)",
    "value": [
        {
            "@odata.etag": "W/\"CQAAABYAAADHcgC8Hl9tRZ/hc1wEUs1TAAAwR4Hg\"",
            "id": "AAMkAGUAAAwTW09AAA=",
            "subject": "You have late tasks!",
            "sender": {
                "emailAddress": {
                    "name": "Microsoft Planner",
                    "address": "noreply@Planner.Office365.com"
                }
            }
        }
    ]
}
```

---

- [graph-rest-beta](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/api/user-list-messages?view=graph-rest-beta&accept=text/markdown)

# Get message - Microsoft Graph v1.0 | Microsoft Learn

Namespace: microsoft.graph

Retrieve the properties and relationships of a [message](resources/message) object.

You can use the `$value` parameter to [get the MIME content of a message](/en-us/graph/outlook-get-mime-message). See also an example below.

There are two scenarios where an app can get a message in another user's mail folder:

- If the app has application permissions, or,
- If the app has the appropriate delegated permissions from one user, and another user has shared a mail folder with that user, or, has given delegated access to that user. See [details and an example](/en-us/graph/outlook-share-messages-folders).

Since the **message** resource supports [extensions](/en-us/graph/extensibility-overview), you can also use the `GET` operation to get custom properties and extension data in a **message** instance.

This API is available in the following [national cloud deployments](/en-us/graph/deployments).

| Global service | US Government L4 | US Government L5 (DOD) | China operated by 21Vianet |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

## Permissions

One of the following permissions is required to call this API. To learn more, including how to choose permissions, see [Permissions](/en-us/graph/permissions-reference).

| Permission type | Permissions (from least to most privileged) |
| --- | --- |
| Delegated (work or school account) | Mail.ReadBasic, Mail.Read |
| Delegated (personal Microsoft account) | Mail.ReadBasic, Mail.Read |
| Application | Mail.ReadBasic.All, Mail.Read |

## HTTP request

To get the specified message:

```http
GET /me/messages/{id}
GET /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages/{id}
GET /me/mailFolders/{id}/messages/{id}
GET /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/mailFolders/{id}/messages/{id}
```

To get the MIME content of the specified message:

```http
GET /me/messages/{id}/$value
GET /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages/{id}/$value
GET /me/mailFolders/{id}/messages/{id}/$value
GET /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/mailFolders/{id}/messages/{id}/$value
```

## Optional query parameters

This method supports the [OData Query Parameters](/en-us/graph/query-parameters) to help customize the response.

Use the `$value` parameter to get the MIME content of a message.

## Request headers

| Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Authorization | string | Bearer {token}. Required. Learn more about [authentication and authorization](/en-us/graph/auth/auth-concepts). |
| Prefer: outlook.body-content-type | string | The format of the **body** and **uniqueBody** properties to be returned in. Values can be "text" or "html". A `Preference-Applied` header is returned as confirmation if this `Prefer` header is specified. If the header is not specified, the **body** and **uniqueBody** properties are returned in HTML format. Optional. |

## Request body

Don't supply a request body for this method.

## Response

If successful, this method returns a `200 OK` response code and [message](resources/message) object in the response body.

Specifying the `$value` parameter returns the message content in MIME format, and not a **message** resource.

## Examples

### Example 1: Get a specific message

#### Request

The following example shows a request.

**HTTP**

```msgraph
GET https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me/messages/AAMkAGVmMDEzMTM4LTZmYWUtNDdkNC1hMDZiLTU1OGY5OTZhYmY4OABGAAAAAAAiQ8W967B7TKBjgx9rVEURBwAiIsqMbYjsT5e-T7KzowPTAAAAAAEMAAAiIsqMbYjsT5e-T7KzowPTAASoXUT3AAA=
```

**C#**

```csharp

// Code snippets are only available for the latest version. Current version is 5.x

// To initialize your graphClient, see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/sdks/create-client?from=snippets&tabs=csharp
var result = await graphClient.Me.Messages["{message-id}"].GetAsync();

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

**Go**

```go

// Code snippets are only available for the latest major version. Current major version is $v1.*

// Dependencies
import (  "context"  msgraphsdk "github.com/microsoftgraph/msgraph-sdk-go"  //other-imports
)

// To initialize your graphClient, see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/sdks/create-client?from=snippets&tabs=go
messages, err := graphClient.Me().Messages().ByMessageId("message-id").Get(context.Background(), nil)

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

**Java**

```java

// Code snippets are only available for the latest version. Current version is 6.x

GraphServiceClient graphClient = new GraphServiceClient(requestAdapter);

Message result = graphClient.me().messages().byMessageId("{message-id}").get();

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

**JavaScript**

```javascript

const options = {authProvider,
};

const client = Client.init(options);

let message = await client.api('/me/messages/AAMkAGVmMDEzMTM4LTZmYWUtNDdkNC1hMDZiLTU1OGY5OTZhYmY4OABGAAAAAAAiQ8W967B7TKBjgx9rVEURBwAiIsqMbYjsT5e-T7KzowPTAAAAAAEMAAAiIsqMbYjsT5e-T7KzowPTAASoXUT3AAA=').get();

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

**PHP**

```php

<?php
use Microsoft\Graph\GraphServiceClient;

$graphServiceClient = new GraphServiceClient($tokenRequestContext, $scopes);

$result = $graphServiceClient->me()->messages()->byMessageId('message-id')->get()->wait();

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

**PowerShell**

```powershell

Import-Module Microsoft.Graph.Mail

# A UPN can also be used as -UserId.
Get-MgUserMessage -UserId $userId -MessageId $messageId

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

**Python**

```python

# Code snippets are only available for the latest version. Current version is 1.x
from msgraph import GraphServiceClient
# To initialize your graph_client, see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/sdks/create-client?from=snippets&tabs=python

result = await graph_client.me.messages.by_message_id('message-id').get()

```

> 
> For details about how to [add the SDK](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdk-installation) to your project and [create an authProvider](/en-us/graph/sdks/choose-authentication-providers) instance, see the [SDK documentation](/en-us/graph/sdks/sdks-overview).

#### Response

The following example shows the response. Note: The response object shown here might be shortened for readability.

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-type: application/json

{
    "@odata.context":"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/$metadata#users('7f180cbb-a5ae-457c-b7e8-6f5b42ba33e7')/messages/$entity",
    "@odata.etag":"W/\"CQAAABYAAAC4ofQHEIqCSbQPot83AFcbAAAnjjuZ\"",
```

Here's a comprehensive summary of the Microsoft Graph API v1.0 endpoints, permissions, and key considerations for Teams channels, chats, and messages:

---

## Sending Messages

### Send a Message to a Channel
You can send a new chatMessage in the specified channel or a chat.

**Endpoint:**
The endpoint should be `https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/teams/{teamId}/channels/{channelId}/messages` where `{teamId}` is the ID of the team and `{channelId}` is the ID of the channel where you want to send the message.

### Send a Message to a Chat
You can send a new message in a chat using:

```
POST https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/chats/{chat-id}/messages
```

---

## Reading Messages

### Get Messages from a Channel
The endpoints are:
`GET /teams/{team-id}/channels/{channel-id}/messages/{message-id}` and `GET /teams/{team-id}/channels/{channel-id}/messages/{message-id}/replies/{reply-id}`

### Get Messages from a Chat
To read messages from group chats or 1:1 chats, use: `GET https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/chats/{chat-id}/messages`. To get the list of chats for a user: `GET https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{user-id}/chats`.

Don't use the `/teams/{team-id}/channels/{channel-id}/messages` endpoint for informal chats — that's only for Teams channels.

---

## Permissions

### For Sending Messages (Channel)
Choose the permission or permissions marked as least privileged for this API. Use a higher privileged permission or permissions only if your app requires it.

Key permissions include:
- **Delegated**: `ChannelMessage.Send`, `Group.ReadWrite.All` (backward compatibility only)
- **Application**: Only for migration scenarios

Application permissions are only supported for migration. Application permissions are ONLY supported when the team is under migration mode. Requests using application-only permissions (not delegated) will fail.

The `Group.ReadWrite.All` permission is supported only for backward compatibility. It's recommended to update your solutions to use an alternative permission.

### For Reading Chat Messages
For background apps, add application permissions: `Chat.Read.All` or `Chat.ReadWrite.All`.

### For Delegated Permissions (Chat)
You need `Chat.Read` or `Chat.ReadWrite` permissions. The signed-in user must be a member of the chat.

---

## Important Limitations

### Throttling
The API is not recommended for data migration. Its throughput is limited to 10 messages every 10 seconds, which isn't sufficient for typical migration scenarios.

### Terms of Use
It is a violation of the terms of use to use Microsoft Teams as a log file. Only send messages that people will read.

### Application-Only Context
For sending messages, you need to use delegated permissions. You can automate with delegated permissions as well. If you want to build an app yourself, you need to use `offline_access` scope in addition to other required scopes. That way when the user signs in and the token is acquired you will get additional refresh tokens which are long lived.

---

Here's a summary of the Microsoft Graph API v1.0 information related to **message rules**, **mailFolder**, and their **list/create/delete permissions**:

---

## Message Rules (messageRule) in Microsoft Graph API v1.0

### List Message Rules

The **List rules** endpoint lets you get all the `messageRule` objects defined for the user's inbox.

**HTTP Request:**

```
GET /me/mailFolders/inbox/messageRules
GET /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/mailFolders/inbox/messageRules
```
This method supports the OData Query Parameters to help customize the response.

**Response:**
If successful, this method returns a `200 OK` response code and a collection of `messageRule` objects in the response body.

**Permissions:**
Choose the permission or permissions marked as least privileged for this API. Use a higher privileged permission or permissions only if your app requires it. The required permissions are **MailboxSettings.Read** (least privileged) or **MailboxSettings.ReadWrite** for both delegated and application permission types.

---

### Create Message Rules

**HTTP Request:**

```
POST /me/mailFolders/inbox/messageRules
POST /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/mailFolders/inbox/messageRules
```
In the request body, supply the parameters that are applicable to your rule.

**Key Request Body Parameters:**
- **actions** – Actions to be taken on a message when the corresponding conditions are fulfilled. Required.
- **conditions** – Conditions that when fulfilled will trigger the corresponding actions for that rule. **sequence** – Indicates the order in which the rule is executed, among other rules. Required.
- You can specify any other writable `messageRule` properties as appropriate in the request body.

**Response:**
If successful, this method returns a `201 Created` response code and a `messageRule` object in the response body.

**Permissions:**
Choose the permission or permissions marked as least privileged for this API. Use a higher privileged permission or permissions only if your app requires it. The required permission is **MailboxSettings.ReadWrite** for both delegated and application scenarios.

---

### Delete Message Rules

The **Delete** endpoint lets you delete the specified `messageRule` object.

**HTTP Request:**
```
DELETE /me/mailFolders/inbox/messageRules/{id}
DELETE /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/mailFolders/inbox/messageRules/{id}
```

**Permissions:**
Choose the permission or permissions marked as least privileged for this API. The required permission is **MailboxSettings.ReadWrite** for both delegated and application scenarios.

---

### Delete mailFolder

Choose the permission or permissions marked as least privileged for this API. Use a higher privileged permission or permissions only if your app requires it. The required permission for deleting a mail folder is **Mail.ReadWrite** for both delegated and application permission types.

---

### General Mail API Notes

- Email messages are represented by the `message` resource and organized in a `mailFolder`.
- You can use well-known folder names such as Inbox, Drafts, SentItems, or DeletedItems to identify certain mail folders that exist by default for all users.
- With the appropriate delegated or application mail permissions, your app can access the mail data of the signed-in user or any user in a tenant.
- In general, do not assume that message and mailFolder IDs are unique and always remain the same within a mailbox. They might change after certain actions such as copy or move.

### Key Sources:
- [List rules](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/api/mailfolder-list-messagerules?view=graph-rest-1.0)
- [Create rule](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/api/mailfolder-post-messagerules?view=graph-rest-1.0)
- [Delete messageRule](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/api/messagerule-delete?view=graph-rest-1.0)

Here is a comprehensive summary of the Microsoft Graph API v1.0 message endpoints for **move**, **copy**, **delete**, and **update** operations, along with their permissions:

---

## 1. Message: Move

The HTTP endpoints are:
- `POST /me/messages/{id}/move`
- `POST /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages/{id}/move`
- `POST /me/mailFolders/{id}/messages/{id}/move`
- `POST /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/mailFolders/{id}/messages/{id}/move`

**Request Body:** Provide a JSON object with a `destinationId` parameter — the destination folder ID, or a well-known folder name. For a list of supported well-known folder names, see the mailFolder resource type.

**Response:** If successful, this method returns a `201 Created` response code and a message resource in the response body.

**Permissions required:** `Mail.ReadWrite` (delegated or application).

---

## 2. Message: Copy

The HTTP endpoints are:
- `POST /me/messages/{id}/copy`
- `POST /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages/{id}/copy`
- `POST /me/mailFolders/{id}/messages/{id}/copy`
- `POST /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/mailFolders/{id}/messages/{id}/copy`

**Request Body:** A JSON object with a `destinationId` parameter (folder ID or well-known folder name).

**Response:** If successful, this method returns a `201 Created` response code and a message resource in the response body.

**Permissions required:** `Mail.ReadWrite` (delegated or application).

---

## 3. Message: Delete

The HTTP endpoints are:
- `DELETE /me/messages/{id}`
- `DELETE /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages/{id}`
- `DELETE /me/mailFolders/{id}/messages/{id}`
- `DELETE /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/mailFolders/{id}/messages/{id}`

**Request Body:** Don't supply a request body for this method.

**Response:** If successful, this method returns a `204 No Content` response code. It doesn't return anything in the response body.

**Permissions required:** `Mail.ReadWrite` (delegated or application). The API requires the `Mail.ReadWrite` permission for deleting messages.

**Important note for application permissions:** You cannot use a token acquired using client_credentials grant type to call the `/me` endpoint. Since you are using application permissions, you need to use `https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{user-id}/messages/{message-id}`.

---

## 4. Message: Update

The HTTP endpoints are:
- `PATCH /me/messages/{id}`
- `PATCH /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/messages/{id}`
- `PATCH /me/mailFolders/{id}/messages/{id}`
- `PATCH /users/{id | userPrincipalName}/mailFolders/{id}/messages/{id}`

**Request Body:** Supply the values for relevant fields that should be updated. Existing properties that are not included in the request body will maintain their previous values or be recalculated based on changes to other property values. For best performance you shouldn't include existing values that haven't changed.

Since the message resource supports extensions, you can use the PATCH operation to add, update, or delete your own app-specific data in custom properties of an extension in an existing message instance.

**Permissions required:** One of the following permissions is required to call this API: `Mail.ReadWrite` (delegated or application).

---

## Summary of Permissions

| Operation | HTTP Method | Permission Required |
|-----------|------------|-------------------|
| **Move**  | `POST .../{id}/move` | `Mail.ReadWrite` |
| **Copy**  | `POST .../{id}/copy` | `Mail.ReadWrite` |
| **Delete**| `DELETE .../{id}` | `Mail.ReadWrite` |
| **Update**| `PATCH .../{id}` | `Mail.ReadWrite` |

All four operations require the **`Mail.ReadWrite`** permission (for both delegated and application access). Administrators can configure application access policy to limit app access to specific mailboxes and not to all the mailboxes in the organization, even if the app has been granted the `Mail.ReadWrite` application permission.

### Additional Notes

- Do not assume that message and mailFolder IDs are unique and always remain the same within a mailbox. They might change after certain actions such as copy or move. You can choose to use immutable IDs to retain the same ID as long as the message remains in the same mailbox.
- You can use well-known folder names such as `Inbox`, `Drafts`, `SentItems`, or `DeletedItems` to identify certain mail folders that exist by default for all users.
